title: National Academy of Medicine

infobox.value: [National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine")
| [John R. Hogness](/wiki/John_R._Hogness "John R. Hogness") | 1970–1974 | first president of the Institute of Medicine[[3]](#cite_note-3)
| [Victor J. Dzau](/wiki/Victor_J._Dzau "Victor J. Dzau") | 2014–present | The Institute of Medicine became the National Academy of Medicine in 2015.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

(**NAM**), known as the **Institute of Medicine** until 2015,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) is an American [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization"), [non-governmental organization](/wiki/NGO "NGO") (though created by and mostly funded by the US government[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]). The National Academy of Medicine is a part of the [National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine"), along with the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences") (NAS), [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering") (NAE), and the [National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine") (NASEM).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American medical society

Not to be confused with [National Academy of Medicine (France)](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Medicine_(France) "National Academy of Medicine (France)").

"Institute of Medicine" redirects here. For other uses, see [Institute of Medicine (disambiguation)](/wiki/Institute_of_Medicine_(disambiguation) "Institute of Medicine (disambiguation)").

National Academy of Medicine

The **National Academy of Medicine** (**NAM**), known as the **Institute of Medicine** until 2015,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) is an American [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization"), [non-governmental organization](/wiki/NGO "NGO") (though created by and mostly funded by the US government[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]). The National Academy of Medicine is a part of the [National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine"), along with the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences") (NAS), [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering") (NAE), and the [National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine") (NASEM).

Operating outside the framework of the United States federal government, it relies on a volunteer workforce of scientists and other experts, operating under a formal [peer-review](/wiki/Peer_review "Peer review") system. As a [national academy](/wiki/National_academy "National academy"), the organization annually elects new members with the help of its existing members; the election is based on the person's distinguished and continuing achievements in a relevant field as well as for their willingness to actively participate.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Medicine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The institute was founded in 1970, under the congressional charter of the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences") as the Institute of Medicine.[[2]](#cite_note-About_the_NAM-2)

On April 28, 2015, NAS membership voted in favor of reconstituting the membership of the IOM as a new National Academy of Medicine and establishing a new division on health and medicine within the NRC that has the program activities of the IOM at its core. These changes took effect on July 1, 2015.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Medicine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Overview")]

The National Academies attempt to obtain authoritative, objective, and scientifically balanced answers to difficult questions of national importance.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The work is conducted by committees of volunteer scientists—leading national and international experts—who serve without compensation. Committees are chosen to assure the requisite expertise and avoid bias or conflict of interest. Every report produced by committee undergoes extensive review and evaluation by a group of external experts who are anonymous to the committee, and whose names are revealed only once the study is published. [Victor Dzau](/wiki/Victor_Dzau "Victor Dzau") is President and Chairman of the Council. His six-year term began on July 1, 2014. The Leonard D. Schaeffer Executive Officer is [J. Michael McGinnis](/wiki/J._Michael_McGinnis "J. Michael McGinnis").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The majority of studies and other activities are requested and funded by the federal government. Private industry, foundations, and state and local governments also initiate studies, as does the academy itself. Reports are made available online for free by the publishing arm of the United States National Academies, the [National Academies Press](/wiki/National_Academies_Press "National Academies Press"), in multiple formats.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The academy is both an honorific membership organization and a policy research organization. Its members, elected on the basis of their professional achievement and commitment to service, serve without compensation in the conduct of studies and other activities on matters of significance to health. Election to active membership is both an honor and a commitment to serve in Institute affairs. The bylaws specify that no more than 80 new members shall be elected annually, including 10 from outside the United States. The announcement of newly elected members occurs at the Annual Meeting in October. As of October 20, 2015, the number of regular members plus international and emeritus members is 2,012.[[2]](#cite_note-About_the_NAM-2) An unusual diversity of talent among NAM members is assured by the charter stipulation that at least one-quarter be selected from outside the health professions, from such fields as the natural, social, and behavioral sciences, as well as law, administration, engineering, and the humanities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* called the NAM (then called the IOM) the United States' "most esteemed and authoritative adviser on issues of health and medicine, and its reports can transform medical thinking around the world".[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### *NAM Perspectives*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Medicine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: NAM Perspectives")]

NAM publishes a weekly periodical, *NAM Perspectives*, described as "a venue for leading health, medical, science, and policy experts to reflect on issues and opportunities important to the advancement of the NAM's mission". Papers present evidence-based descriptions and individual viewpoints on strategies and priorities, and must be accessible to a broad audience.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Medicine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Awards")]

The [Rhoda and Bernard Sarnat International Prize in Mental Health](/wiki/Sarnat_Prize "Sarnat Prize") (Sarnat Prize) was established in 1992 and is awarded annually by the Academy to recognize individuals, groups, or organizations for outstanding achievement in improving mental health. It is accompanied by a medal and $20,000.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Notable members, past and present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Medicine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notable members, past and present")]

* [Harold Amos](/wiki/Harold_Amos "Harold Amos"), microbiologist and professor
* [Nancy Andrews](/wiki/Nancy_Andrews_(biologist) "Nancy Andrews (biologist)"), Dean of [Duke University School of Medicine](/wiki/Duke_University_School_of_Medicine "Duke University School of Medicine")
* [Andrea Baccarelli](/wiki/Andrea_Baccarelli "Andrea Baccarelli"), Professor and Chair of Environmental Health Sciences at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") and President of the International Society of Environmental Epidemiology.
* [Elizabeth Blackburn](/wiki/Elizabeth_Blackburn "Elizabeth Blackburn"), biologist and winner of 2009 [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") for co-discovery of [telomerase](/wiki/Telomerase "Telomerase")
* [Patricia Flatley Brennan](/wiki/Patricia_Flatley_Brennan "Patricia Flatley Brennan"), Director of the [National Library of Medicine](/wiki/National_Library_of_Medicine "National Library of Medicine")
* [Emery N. Brown](/wiki/Emery_N._Brown "Emery N. Brown"), statistician, neuroscientist, and anesthesiologist, Director of the [Harvard–MIT Program of Health Sciences and Technology](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93MIT_Program_of_Health_Sciences_and_Technology "Harvard–MIT Program of Health Sciences and Technology")
* [Namandjé Bumpus](/wiki/Namandj%C3%A9_Bumpus "Namandjé Bumpus"), pharmacologist and the Chief Scientist of the [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration")
* [Atul Butte](/wiki/Atul_Butte "Atul Butte"), pediatrician and scientist at the [University of California, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco")
* [Robert Califf](/wiki/Robert_Califf "Robert Califf"), cardiologist, [FDA](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration") Deputy Commissioner
* [Ben Carson](/wiki/Ben_Carson "Ben Carson"), columnist and retired American neurosurgeon, former director of pediatric neurosurgery at [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital")
* [Anthony Cerami](/wiki/Anthony_Cerami "Anthony Cerami"), pioneering medical researcher
* [Dennis S. Charney](/wiki/Dennis_S._Charney "Dennis S. Charney"), dean of the [Mount Sinai School of Medicine](/wiki/Icahn_School_of_Medicine_at_Mount_Sinai "Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai") in New York City
* [Sarah Cleaveland](/wiki/Sarah_Cleaveland "Sarah Cleaveland"), veterinary epidemiologist
* [Jewel Plummer Cobb](/wiki/Jewel_Plummer_Cobb "Jewel Plummer Cobb"), cell biologist and President of [California State University, Fullerton](/wiki/California_State_University,_Fullerton "California State University, Fullerton"), 1981–90
* [Francis Collins](/wiki/Francis_Collins_(geneticist) "Francis Collins (geneticist)"), geneticist, leader in the [Human Genome Project](/wiki/Human_Genome_Project "Human Genome Project") and Director of [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health")
* [Jim Collins](/wiki/James_Collins_(bioengineer) "James Collins (bioengineer)"), synthetic biology pioneer and [MacArthur](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows_Program "MacArthur Fellows Program") genius
* [Toby Cosgrove](/wiki/Toby_Cosgrove "Toby Cosgrove"), cardiothoracic surgeon, inventor, and CEO, [Cleveland Clinic](/wiki/Cleveland_Clinic "Cleveland Clinic")
* [Mark Daly](/wiki/Mark_Daly_(scientist) "Mark Daly (scientist)"), statistician and human geneticist, professor of genetics at [Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School")
* [Kenneth L. Davis](/wiki/Kenneth_L._Davis "Kenneth L. Davis"), author, medical researcher and CEO of Mount Sinai Medical Center
* [Anthony Fauci](/wiki/Anthony_Fauci "Anthony Fauci") Lienhard Award for Decades of Work Improving Public Health and Leadership in Shaping COVID-19 Pandemic Response
* [Stanton A. Glantz](/wiki/Stanton_Glantz "Stanton Glantz"), Professor of Medicine (UCSF) and prominent tobacco control researcher and activist
* [Shimon Glick](/wiki/Shimon_Glick "Shimon Glick"), [Ben Gurion University](/wiki/Ben-Gurion_University_of_the_Negev "Ben-Gurion University of the Negev"), endocrinology, internal medicine, medical education and medical ethics
* [Farshid Guilak](/wiki/Farshid_Guilak "Farshid Guilak"), Biomedical engineering and orthopaedic researcher, [Shriners Hospitals for Children](/wiki/Shriners_Hospitals_for_Children "Shriners Hospitals for Children") and [Washington University in St. Louis](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis")
* [Margaret Hamburg](/wiki/Margaret_Hamburg "Margaret Hamburg"), commissioner of the U.S. Food and Drug Administration
* [Mary Hawn](/wiki/Mary_Hawn "Mary Hawn"), chair of surgery at [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University")
* [Maurice Hilleman](/wiki/Maurice_Hilleman "Maurice Hilleman"), microbiologist
* [Anna Huttenlocher](/wiki/Anna_Huttenlocher "Anna Huttenlocher"), a rheumatologist and cell biologist
* [David Ho](/wiki/David_Ho_(scientist) "David Ho (scientist)"), a pioneer in the use of [protease inhibitors](/wiki/Protease_inhibitor_(pharmacology) "Protease inhibitor (pharmacology)") in treating [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV")-infected patients
* [Leroy Hood](/wiki/Leroy_Hood "Leroy Hood"), winner of the 2003 [Lemelson–MIT Prize](/wiki/Lemelson%E2%80%93MIT_Prize "Lemelson–MIT Prize")
* [Harold Jaffe](/wiki/Harold_Jaffe_(epidemiologist) "Harold Jaffe (epidemiologist)"), physician, epidemiologist, and early HIV/AIDS researcher[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [Arthur Kellermann](/wiki/Arthur_Kellermann "Arthur Kellermann"), professor and founding chairman of the department of Emergency Medicine at [Emory University](/wiki/Emory_University "Emory University")
* [Herbert Kleber](/wiki/Herbert_Kleber "Herbert Kleber"), professor of psychiatry, [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University")
* [Philip J. Landrigan](/wiki/Philip_J._Landrigan "Philip J. Landrigan"), pediatrician and children's environmental health advocate
* [Jeffrey Lieberman](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lieberman "Jeffrey Lieberman"), chair of psychiatry, [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"); president, [American Psychiatric Association](/wiki/American_Psychiatric_Association "American Psychiatric Association")
* [Rudolph Leibel](/wiki/Rudolph_Leibel "Rudolph Leibel"), MD, professor at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") and discoverer of [leptin](/wiki/Leptin "Leptin") and [leptin receptor](/wiki/Leptin_receptor "Leptin receptor")[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Alice H. Lichtenstein](/wiki/Alice_H._Lichtenstein "Alice H. Lichtenstein"), senior scientist and director of Cardiovascular Nutrition Laboratory at [Human Nutrition Research Center on Aging](/wiki/Human_Nutrition_Research_Center_on_Aging "Human Nutrition Research Center on Aging"), professor at [Tufts University](/wiki/Tufts_University "Tufts University")
* [Susan Lindquist](/wiki/Susan_Lindquist "Susan Lindquist"), a molecular biologist and former Director of the [Whitehead Institute](/wiki/Whitehead_Institute "Whitehead Institute")
* [Howard Markel](/wiki/Howard_Markel "Howard Markel"), George E. Wantz Distinguished Professor of the History of Medicine and Director of the Center for the History of Medicine at the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan")
* [Jonna Mazet](/wiki/Jonna_Mazet "Jonna Mazet"), professor of epidemiology at [UC Davis School of Veterinary Medicine](/wiki/UC_Davis_School_of_Veterinary_Medicine "UC Davis School of Veterinary Medicine") and executive director of the [One Health Institute](/wiki/One_Health_Institute "One Health Institute")
* [Maclyn McCarty](/wiki/Maclyn_McCarty "Maclyn McCarty"), youngest member of the research team responsible for the [Avery–MacLeod–McCarty experiment](/wiki/Avery%E2%80%93MacLeod%E2%80%93McCarty_experiment "Avery–MacLeod–McCarty experiment")
* [Sherilyn S. McCoy](/wiki/Sherilyn_S._McCoy "Sherilyn S. McCoy"), CEO of [Avon Products](/wiki/Avon_Products "Avon Products") and former Vice Chairman of [Johnson & Johnson](/wiki/Johnson_%26_Johnson "Johnson & Johnson")
* [Ruslan Medzhitov](/wiki/Ruslan_Medzhitov "Ruslan Medzhitov"), professor of [immunobiology](/wiki/Immunobiology "Immunobiology") at [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), co-discoverer of human [Toll-like receptors](/wiki/Toll-like_receptors "Toll-like receptors") (TLRs)
* [David O. Meltzer](/wiki/David_O._Meltzer "David O. Meltzer"), Professor of Medicine and health economist at [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago")
* [Mario J. Molina](/wiki/Mario_J._Molina "Mario J. Molina"), recipient of the 1995 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") for discovery of impact of [CFCs](/wiki/Chlorofluorocarbon "Chlorofluorocarbon") on [ozone layer](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer")
* [Sean J. Morrison](/wiki/Sean_J._Morrison "Sean J. Morrison"), stem cell biologist and director of the Children's Medical Center Research Institute at [UT Southwestern Medical Center](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Southwestern_Medical_Center "University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center")
* [Herbert Needleman](/wiki/Herbert_Needleman "Herbert Needleman"), pediatrician and psychiatrist
* [Carl F. Nathan](/wiki/Carl_F._Nathan "Carl F. Nathan"), Professor of Immunology and Microbial [Pathogenesis](/wiki/Pathogenesis "Pathogenesis") at [Weill Cornell Medicine](/wiki/Weill_Cornell_Medicine "Weill Cornell Medicine")
* [Peter R. Orszag](/wiki/Peter_R._Orszag "Peter R. Orszag"), 37th Director of the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget") under President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")
* [Nicholas A. Peppas](/wiki/Nicholas_A._Peppas "Nicholas A. Peppas"), pioneer of [biomaterials](/wiki/Biomaterial "Biomaterial") and [drug delivery](/wiki/Drug_delivery "Drug delivery")
* [Megan Ranney](/wiki/Megan_Ranney "Megan Ranney"), emergency physician, deputy dean of the [Brown University School of Public Health](/wiki/Brown_University_School_of_Public_Health "Brown University School of Public Health"), public health leader and communicator[[15]](#cite_note-NAM20221017-15)
* [Frederick Redlich](/wiki/Frederick_Redlich "Frederick Redlich"), dean of the [Yale School of Medicine](/wiki/Yale_School_of_Medicine "Yale School of Medicine") from 1967 to 1972
* [James Rothman](/wiki/James_Rothman "James Rothman"), winner of the 2002 [Albert Lasker Award for Basic Medical Research](/wiki/Albert_Lasker_Award_for_Basic_Medical_Research "Albert Lasker Award for Basic Medical Research")
* [Charles Rotimi](/wiki/Charles_Rotimi "Charles Rotimi"), epidemiologist and Chief & Senior Investigator at the [National Human Genome Research Institute](/wiki/National_Human_Genome_Research_Institute "National Human Genome Research Institute")[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* [Jeffrey Sachs](/wiki/Jeffrey_Sachs "Jeffrey Sachs"), economist and director of [The Earth Institute](/wiki/The_Earth_Institute "The Earth Institute") at Columbia University
* [David A. Savitz](/wiki/David_A._Savitz "David A. Savitz"), director of the Disease Prevention and Public Health Institute at the Mount Sinai Medical Center
* [Richard A. Smith](/wiki/Richard_A._Smith_(physician) "Richard A. Smith (physician)"), physician
* [Shirley M. Tilghman](/wiki/Shirley_M._Tilghman "Shirley M. Tilghman"), former president of [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University")
* [Abraham Verghese](/wiki/Abraham_Verghese "Abraham Verghese"), novelist and recipient of the [National Humanities Medal](/wiki/National_Humanities_Medal "National Humanities Medal")
* [Mary Wakefield](/wiki/Mary_Wakefield "Mary Wakefield"), appointed administrator of the [Health Resources and Services Administration](/wiki/Health_Resources_and_Services_Administration "Health Resources and Services Administration") (HRSA) by President Barack Obama in February 2009
* [Douglas C. Wallace](/wiki/Douglas_C._Wallace "Douglas C. Wallace"), geneticist and pioneer of [human mitochondrial genetics](/wiki/Human_mitochondrial_genetics "Human mitochondrial genetics")
* [Lawrence Weed](/wiki/Lawrence_Weed "Lawrence Weed"), creator of the problem-oriented [medical record](/wiki/Medical_record "Medical record")
* [Sheldon Weinbaum](/wiki/Sheldon_Weinbaum "Sheldon Weinbaum"), biomedical engineer, [biofluid](/wiki/Body_fluid "Body fluid") mechanician and Distinguished Professor, Emeritus, at the [City College of New York](/wiki/City_College_of_New_York "City College of New York")
* [Ben Weston](/wiki/Ben_Weston_(physician) "Ben Weston (physician)"), Chief Health Policy Advisor for [Milwaukee County](/wiki/Milwaukee_County "Milwaukee County")
* [Kern Wildenthal](/wiki/Kern_Wildenthal "Kern Wildenthal"), former president of the [University of Texas Southwestern Medical School](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Southwestern_Medical_School "University of Texas Southwestern Medical School")
* [William Julius Wilson](/wiki/William_Julius_Wilson "William Julius Wilson"), sociologist
* [Elias Zerhouni](/wiki/Elias_Zerhouni "Elias Zerhouni"), former executive vice-dean of [Johns Hopkins School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine") and director of the National Institutes of Health under [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush")
